sourceMovie.title: Lincoln
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Audiences latched onto Lincoln (2012) as a history theatrical experience: big enough for a group watch, specific enough to reward a second viewing once you know where it's headed. The history and drama blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: The Patriot
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Lincoln who respond to its history will find The Patriot (2000) immediately familiar. Both films share a drama sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: Selma
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason Lincoln fans keep landing on Selma (2014) — the history is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: Flags of Our Fathers
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Flags of Our Fathers draws from the same history well as Lincoln, channeling drama in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 2006 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: Glory
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Glory (1989) captures the history appeal of Lincoln without simply imitating it. The drama streak running through both films is what puts Glory at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: Free State of Jones
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The history appeal of Lincoln — that tense edge, the drama — translates cleanly into Free State of Jones (2016). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: Emancipation
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind Emancipation (2022) clearly understood what makes history work — the same quality that keeps Lincoln rewatchable. Expect the same tense rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: W.
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: W. (2008) is the kind of film Lincoln fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The history overlap is real, but it's the tense emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: Breach
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Breach shares Lincoln's gift for history — the 2007 film delivers drama without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: Worth
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes Lincoln compelling — the history, the drama — shows up in Worth (2021) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that tense storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

recommendations.title: Bloody Sunday
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The history energy of Lincoln is alive in Bloody Sunday (2002), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a tense payoff that rivals the original pick.

faq.question: What should I watch after Lincoln?
faq.answer: Try The Patriot, Selma, Flags of Our Fathers—each shares DNA with Lincoln while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Lincoln?
faq.answer: Lincoln (2012) is best described as History, Drama. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Lincoln worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 6.8/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this history lane.
